memories.memory_text: I kicked the table so hard my foot stung—this whole mess with replacing Rainbow was making me sick, and I couldn’t stop picturing squeezing his neck until his eyes popped out.
grounded_facts: Nora kicks the table in frustration during the meeting.
Nora expresses violent fantasy about Rainbow Randolph choking him and describing his eyes popping out.

memories.memory_text: When Frank mentioned Sheldon Mopes, I actually laughed—Smoochy the Rhino? The guy was a joke, stuck doing hospital gigs, and the idea of building a show around him felt like surrender.
grounded_facts: Nora laughs in response to Sheldon Mopes being suggested.
Nora refers to Sheldon Mopes as 'Smoochy the Rhino' and calls him a sap and a joke.
Nora expresses disdain for hiring Sheldon, citing his lack of success and appeal.

memories.scene_title: 12、EXT. CONEY ISLAND BOARDWALK - NATHAN'S HOT DOGS - SHORT
memories.memory_text: I watched Sheldon shove that gross soy dog into his mouth, talking with food flying, and then he yanked Smoochy’s busted head out of a bag like it was some holy relic—screaming about integrity like anyone at Kidnet actually cared.
grounded_facts: Nora observes Sheldon eating a sloppily-garnished soy hot dog at Nathan's.
Sheldon speaks passionately about integrity and doing good work while holding the Smoochy head.
Nora responds with 'Believe it or not, no,' 'I haven't the foggiest,' and 'I can see that,' indicating restrained acknowledgment.

memories.scene_title: 15、INT. NORA BISHOP'S OFFICE - CLOSE ON LITTLE MECHANICAL
memories.memory_text: I ignored Sheldon’s raised hand and kept going with the recap—ice pops yes, string cheese no, shampoo split still up in the air. He wasn’t going to derail this meeting with his sanctimonious act.
grounded_facts: Nora continued the meeting without acknowledging Sheldon's raised hand.
She reviewed merchandising decisions: approved Smoochy ice pops, rejected string cheese, and noted ongoing negotiations over shampoo.

memories.scene_title: 15、INT. NORA BISHOP'S OFFICE - CLOSE ON LITTLE MECHANICAL
memories.memory_text: After I tossed the Frisbee into the hall and Sheldon went to get it, I told John to shut the door—and then lock it. The click was quiet, but it made the point.
grounded_facts: Nora threw a Smoochy Frisbee into the hallway.
She instructed a staffer named John to shut and then lock the door after Sheldon left the room.
The door was closed and locked while Sheldon was outside.

memories.memory_text: I stopped walking, looked Sheldon in the eye, and told him straight—his being on TV had nothing to do with talent or value, only because Rainbow Randolph was a scumbag; I delivered him like a bag of groceries, and he should stop pretending he mattered more than that.
grounded_facts: Nora stops walking and looks Sheldon in the eye for the first time in the scene.
Nora explicitly states that Sheldon is on TV only because Rainbow Randolph is a degenerate scumbag.
Nora compares delivering Sheldon to delivering a bag of groceries.
Nora dismisses Sheldon's emotional appeal by saying she has more emotional investment in her nail polish.
Nora commands Sheldon to stop 'peddling his sap' and reduces his role to 'smile and nod your head.'

memories.memory_text: I told Sheldon the 'Please and Thank You Song' was cut because it's sappy and eats into the 'Cookie Song'—then I ordered him to reinstate the original lyrics, the ones glorifying sugar. He called me out, said I see kids as wallets with pigtails. It stung, but I couldn't show it.
grounded_facts: Nora ordered the removal of the 'Please and Thank You Song' and demanded the original 'Cookie Song' lyrics be restored.
Sheldon objected on moral grounds, arguing the 'Cookie Song' lacked educational value without the coda.
Nora justified the decision by stating the network exists to sell sugar and plastic, not promote healthy values.
Sheldon accused Nora of viewing children as profit sources, which visibly affected her.

memories.memory_text: I was ready to roll my eyes at another grotesque puppet routine, but then I saw the kids—real laughter, real joy, their faces lit up like they actually believed in that stupid Magic Cookie Bag.
grounded_facts: Nora observes the children in the bleachers during the Smoochy Show taping.
The children are laughing and cheering enthusiastically.
Nora experiences a shift in perception, noticing the genuine emotional connection between Smoochy and the children.
This moment contrasts with her usual cynical and dismissive attitude toward the show.

memories.scene_title: 25、INT. FRANK STOKES' OFFICE - TIGHT ON NEW YORK POST FRONT
memories.memory_text: I told Burke there was no excuse for him, and then I reminded him that we were the ones who dug Sheldon up and handed him his show when no one else would.
grounded_facts: Nora says 'There's no excuse for you' directly to Burke.
Nora states that she and others found Sheldon, revived his career, and gave him the Smoochy show when he had no prospects.

memories.memory_text: I told him 'no' when he asked me to lunch, and then made it clear I wouldn't change my mind — he didn't get to waltz in here acting like we were going to be partners after everything.
grounded_facts: Sheldon invited Nora to lunch as a gesture of cooperation.
Nora responded with a blunt 'No' and followed it with 'I won't' when Sheldon left the offer open.

memories.memory_text: He tried to make small talk on his way out, muttering about the plant like it meant something — I didn’t even look up, just let him walk out alone.
grounded_facts: Sheldon commented on a ficus plant while leaving Nora's office.
Nora ignored him completely as he exited, maintaining silence and avoiding engagement.

memories.memory_text: I walked in and threw the paper on his desk—'Congratulations on your little ice show'—and watched his face go blank. He had the nerve to look confused after selling out like that.
grounded_facts: Nora enters the apartment holding the daily news.
She speaks sarcastically to Sheldon about his ice show.
She tosses the newspaper onto his desk.
She confronts him about abandoning his integrity.
She exits before he responds.

memories.memory_text: I saw him standing there in that stupid knit cap and sunglasses, like he could just hide from what he'd done—like I wouldn't recognize the guy who performed in front of a fifty-foot swastika.
grounded_facts: Sheldon approached Nora while wearing a knit cap and sunglasses to disguise himself.
Nora saw a photo of Sheldon at a rally with a large swastika.
Nora rejected Sheldon's claim that he didn't know it was a Nazi event.

memories.memory_text: He brought up the balcony, that stupid dance—like any of it meant something. I told him I was drunk. It was easier than saying none of it ever mattered.
grounded_facts: Sheldon referenced shared intimate moments, including the balcony and the Klunky-Wunky dance.
Nora dismissed those moments as insignificant, claiming she was drunk.

memories.memory_text: I remember the way he just walked in like he owned the place, tossing around that smug 'R man is back' crap while I was still reeling from the Smoochy mess. When he put his hands on me and tried to kiss me, I shoved him hard—felt the couch rattle when he nearly went over the table.
grounded_facts: Randolph entered Nora's apartment uninvited and sat on her couch.
He placed his hands on her shoulders and attempted to kiss her.
Nora physically shoved him, causing him to nearly fall over the coffee table.

memories.memory_text: I backed him into the wall and asked point-blank if he set Sheldon up. He didn’t answer—just stared at me, then called me a bitch. That’s when I punched him in the jaw. I felt it connect, solid.
grounded_facts: Nora confronted Randolph about setting up Sheldon.
Randolph did not answer and instead insulted her.
Nora punched Randolph in the jaw.

memories.memory_text: I walked into Spinner Dunn's, scanned the room, and asked Tommy and Spinner if Burke had been in—my voice was sharp, impatient, because I needed to find him. When I said it was about Sheldon, Spinner suddenly stopped banging his head and looked up, saying my brother's name like he actually remembered him.
grounded_facts: Nora entered Spinner Dunn's nightclub at night.
She addressed Tommy and Spinner, asking if Burke had been in.
She stated she needed to talk to Burke about Sheldon.
Spinner reacted to the mention of Sheldon by lifting his head and speaking his name.

memories.memory_text: I stood there beside Sheldon as the drumroll echoed, and when he said he wouldn’t be back without me, I finally admitted I was sorry—really sorry—for not believing him, for getting caught up in all that anti-Smoochy noise.
grounded_facts: Nora was present backstage with Sheldon before his performance.
Sheldon told Nora he wouldn't be back if it wasn't for her.
Nora apologized to Sheldon for not believing him and for participating in the anti-Smoochy movement.
A low tympani roll was heard during the exchange.
A stagehand interrupted to inform Sheldon he was needed on stage.

memories.scene_title: 79、INT. SHELDON'S DRESSING ROOM - AFTER THE SHOW
memories.memory_text: I stood there trying to find the words, my voice quiet but insistent—I just needed to know where we stood, after everything. When I said it was about us, I saw the shift in his face, that flicker of discomfort, and I knew I’d crossed into something raw.
grounded_facts: Nora initiates a conversation with Sheldon about their relationship.
She explicitly states she needs clarification about 'us'.
She references her mistaken belief about Sheldon's ties to Neo-Nazis as a past obstacle.

memories.scene_title: 79、INT. SHELDON'S DRESSING ROOM - AFTER THE SHOW
memories.memory_text: One second I was bracing for another deflection, and the next he was on me—kissing me hard, no warning. I didn’t hesitate; I tore at his costume, pulled him down, and let everything else burn away in that mess on the floor.
grounded_facts: Sheldon suddenly grabs and kisses Nora without warning.
Nora actively participates by ripping off his costume and falling to the floor with him.
The interaction shifts abruptly from emotional tension to physical intimacy.

memories.memory_text: I had my arm around Sheldon while he stood there staring at Spinner in the casket, barely holding it together. He kept saying it was his fault, like he could’ve stopped any of it.
grounded_facts: Nora has her arm around Sheldon at the wake.
Sheldon blames himself for Spinner's death.
Nora attempts to console Sheldon by telling him he couldn't have prevented it.

memories.memory_text: I started singing 'The Last Nappy Time' quietly, the one Rickets the Hippo did—sang it because it got me through my mom’s death. Sheldon joined in, and then we just… held each other while the bagpipes played.
grounded_facts: Nora mentions 'The Last Nappy Time' as a song that comforted her after her mother's death.
Nora sings the song quietly, and Sheldon joins her.
Nora and Sheldon embrace after singing together.

memories.memory_text: I heard Tommy say it wasn't Smiley who killed Spinner — it was Parade of Hope. When he named Merv Green, something in me snapped, like all the lies I’d swallowed finally had a face.
grounded_facts: Nora was present at the ice rink when Tommy revealed that Merv Green, not Rainbow Randolph, was responsible for Spinner Dunn's murder.
Tommy attributed the information to an investigation involving his cousin Ian from the morgue, who identified the neck injury as Parade of Hope's signature.
Nora directly asked 'Merv Green?' in response, indicating recognition and shock.

memories.memory_text: I remember the way Merv Green grabbed me at that 'Feed the Children' benefit—smiling like a saint while his hand squeezed my ass. I knew right then he was a scumbag.
grounded_facts: Nora was groped by Merv Green at a charity event.
She formed a negative opinion of Merv immediately based on that incident.

memories.memory_text: I stood in the warehouse and watched Danny raise the hatchet while Merv begged. I didn’t look away when the screaming stopped.
grounded_facts: Nora was present in the warehouse during Merv Green’s execution.
She witnessed Danny advance with a hatchet and heard Merv’s final screams before they abruptly ended.

memories.memory_text: I was reorganizing the schedule cards when he came up behind me, ripped a Smoochy card off the board, and shoved it in my face, talking about a 'rhino problem' and deals in his culture.
grounded_facts: Nora was alone in Stokes' office, rearranging cards on the cork board displaying the network schedule.
Chick entered with two others, walked to the board, and removed a Smoochy index card.
Chick waved the card in Nora's face and made a threat referencing cultural expectations and consequences.

memories.memory_text: He pulled a switchblade out of nowhere and held it to my throat—cold metal against my skin—then said they'd kill everyone at the network, starting with the pretty ones, before throwing it into a Smoochy card.
grounded_facts: Chick suddenly produced a switchblade and held it to Nora's throat.
Nora felt the physical presence of the blade at her neck.
Chick threatened to kill people at the network, specifically 'the pretty ones,' then threw the knife into a Smoochy card on the board.

memories.memory_text: I told Sheldon the truth about Stokes trying to replace him with Takashi, that smug international puppet, but before I could make him see how serious it was, everything fell apart.
grounded_facts: Nora revealed Stokes' plan to replace Sheldon with Takashi.
She references Takashi being a popular children's host from Asia, implying prior knowledge.
Sheldon was initially stunned and overwhelmed by the information.

memories.memory_text: Randolph burst in yelling about us being together, waving that damn gun, and suddenly I was on the defensive about Jingle Jackson and Johnny—like my past was some punchline he got to expose.
grounded_facts: Randolph interrupted the conversation with a gun and emotional accusations about his past relationship with Nora.
Nora reacted with visible shame when her past relationships with children's hosts were brought up.
She admitted to being a 'kiddie-host groupie' but insisted her past was not Sheldon's business.

memories.scene_title: 95、INT. SHELDON'S FOYER - TIGHT ON REVOLVER - SHORT WHILE
memories.memory_text: I handed Randolph tea, then asked Sheldon what he thought he was doing with that gun — he just kept loading it like I wasn’t even there.
grounded_facts: Nora handed Randolph a cup of tea.
Nora asked Sheldon, 'What are you doing?' in reference to him loading a revolver.
Sheldon continued loading the gun without responding to Nora's question initially.

memories.scene_title: 105、INT. MADISON SQUARE GARDEN - FEW MINUTES LATER
memories.memory_text: I was still watching the opera, caught up in the stupid pageantry, when something in the way Stokes and Burke kept glancing at each other made my gut tighten—like they weren’t just nervous, like they *knew* something was coming.
grounded_facts: Nora was watching the performance and was described as 'totally entranced.'
Stokes and Burke appeared on edge, with Burke checking his watch, which Nora could observe from the network box.

memories.scene_title: 105、INT. MADISON SQUARE GARDEN - FEW MINUTES LATER
memories.memory_text: I didn’t see the rifle at first, but once I did—up on the catwalk, Buggy hunched over it—I bolted from the booth, screaming Sheldon’s name, my voice cracking as I ran for the rink stairs.
grounded_facts: Buggy was loading and aiming a rifle from the catwalk, visible above the arena.
Nora transitions from observer to intervenor, attempting to reach Sheldon after realizing the threat.
The script implies a shift in Nora’s focus from passive viewing to urgent action.

memories.memory_text: I finally reached Sheldon through the chaos, smoke stinging my eyes, and grabbed him—told him Burke was with Stokes, that he’d been in it all along.
grounded_facts: Nora reaches Sheldon amidst ongoing chaos at the rink.
She delivers the information that Burke is aligned with Stokes and has been involved from the beginning.
She initiates physical contact by embracing Sheldon.

memories.memory_text: I shouted 'No, Sheldon, don't!' as he pulled the gun from his costume—my voice cracked, but he didn’t stop, just ran into the smoke after Burke.
grounded_facts: Sheldon retrieves a gun from inside his costume after hearing Nora’s warning.
Nora verbally intervenes, pleading with Sheldon not to use violence.
Sheldon ignores her plea and runs toward Burke, leaving Nora behind.

memories.memory_text: I picked up the gun and told Burke to let Sheldon go, or I'd pump one in that Grecian Formula scalp of his.
grounded_facts: Nora retrieves Sheldon's gun after it falls during the fight.
She points the gun at Burke and commands him to release Sheldon.
She threatens Burke with the phrase 'pump one in that Grecian Formula scalp of yours.'

memories.memory_text: After the shots rang out from behind us, I saw the fear in Sheldon's eyes, so I told him it was just a bus backfire and patted his back—like I could make it okay.
grounded_facts: Two gunshots are heard after Sheldon and Nora leave the alley. Sheldon reacts to the sound with visible distress. Nora reassures him by attributing the sound to a bus backfire and touches his back.

memories.memory_text: I called him 'Smoochy' and told him it was time to go home—like it was just another end to a long day, even though everything had changed.
grounded_facts: Nora says 'Let's go home, Smoochy. Show's over.'
This occurs on the street after the events at the rink
She uses a nickname she previously used for Sheldon, indicating familiarity and emotional shift

memories.memory_text: We kissed—really kissed—and I didn’t pull away, even with the music swelling and the world blurring around us. I let it happen.
grounded_facts: Nora and Sheldon share a passionate kiss
The kiss occurs immediately after dialogue between them
Music swells and camera moves away, suggesting emotional significance

memories.memory_text: I was dancing through Rainbowland in my yellow bow tie, singing 'Friends Come In All Sizes' with the Krinkle Kids, and every voice rose with mine on that big finish — I could feel the joy blasting out of me like a rainbow cannon.
grounded_facts: Randolph performed 'Friends Come In All Sizes' on a children's show with the Krinkle Kids.
He wore a yellow bow tie and danced through a set called Rainbowland.
Angelo Pike, a Krinkle Kid, danced behind him during the performance.
The performance ended with a big, energetic finish to the song.

memories.memory_text: I took a gulp of my Scotch, opened the briefcase, and saw five grand staring me in the face—cold, crisp bills that meant I could breathe for one more night.
grounded_facts: Randolph drank Scotch before opening the briefcase. The briefcase contained five thousand dollars in cash. He opened the briefcase at the table with the couple. He made the decision to take the money. He slammed the briefcase shut after being questioned.

memories.memory_text: I turned to leave with the briefcase, and suddenly they were screaming at me with guns drawn—'Freeze, you cocksucker!'—then the feds poured in from every direction, surrounding me like I was some kind of animal.
grounded_facts: Randolph began walking away with the briefcase. The husband and wife revealed themselves as federal agents by drawing guns. The husband yelled 'Freeze

you cocksucker!' and the wife ordered him to drop the briefcase. Federal agents stormed the bar and surrounded Randolph. The arrest occurred immediately after he took the briefcase and turned to leave.

memories.memory_text: I stood there in the cold dawn light, watching newspapers spill onto the street like garbage, each one screaming my name in those ugly bold headlines—'Rainbow Randolph Busted'—like I was some common criminal.
grounded_facts: Newspapers with headlines about Randolph's bribery scandal are being delivered to the curb in Times Square.
Headlines explicitly name Randolph in connection with a bribery scandal.
The scene takes place at dawn, implying a quiet, exposed moment.

memories.memory_text: I stood there watching them roll up the backdrops and haul away the rainbow—my rainbow—while some grunt swept up the glitter like it was just trash.
grounded_facts: The Rainbow Randolph/Krinkle Kid set is being dismantled.
Backdrops are rolled up.
The giant rainbow centerpiece is wheeled off.
Workers sweep up glittery 'magic Rainbow dust.'

memories.memory_text: I shouted in the rain, 'I was Rainbow fucking Randolph!'—my voice cracking as I tried to make Frank see who I used to be, how the world stopped when I walked in.
grounded_facts: Randolph shouted "I was Rainbow fucking Randolph!" during a confrontation with Stokes on a rain-soaked dock.
He referenced his past fame
listing luxuries and public adoration to assert his former status.
His voice rose progressively before culminating in a shout
indicating emotional escalation.

memories.memory_text: I grabbed Frank's coat and begged him to get my slot back. When he left, I shouted that the wheels were turning, then muttered to myself about clean men falling into mud.
grounded_facts: Randolph clung to Stokes' lapels and pleaded for help restoring his job.
He referred to Smoochy as a 'horned carpetbagger' and emphasized their shared history.
After being rejected, he shouted that 'the wheels are turning'.
He later muttered quietly to himself that even a clean man falls into mud.

memories.memory_text: I stood there on the dock, just me and the river, no one answering, no one listening—again.
grounded_facts: Randolph is physically alone on the dock.
The scene ends with a fade out, emphasizing isolation.

memories.memory_text: They replaced me with that rhino freak because they called me a degenerate scumbag — like I’m nothing, like I didn’t build that show.
grounded_facts: Randolph was replaced on the show by Sheldon (Smoochy the Rhino).
Nora explicitly states that Sheldon is only on TV because Rainbow Randolph is a 'degenerate scumbag'.
Randolph is not present in the scene, but the dialogue directly references his disgrace as the reason for Sheldon's presence.

memories.memory_text: I saw Burke walk out after shaking hands with that rhino freak, and when I confronted him, he looked me dead in the eye and called me a cigarette butt—just tossed me aside like I was nothing.
grounded_facts: Randolph observes Burke and Sheldon shaking hands through a restaurant window.
Randolph hides in the shadows as Burke exits the club.
Randolph confronts Burke, asserting that Burke is his agent.
Burke responds that Randolph is 'a cigarette butt' and that their professional relationship is over.
Burke walks away after dismissing Randolph.

memories.memory_text: I slipped my homemade cookies into Smoochy’s magic bag and tossed his stupid little treats in the trash—let him choke on what I left behind.
grounded_facts: Randolph entered the prop room and located Smoochy's magic cookie bag on the prop shelf. He removed the original cookies and replaced them with his own homemade ones. He expressed amusement while doing so
chuckling and making a mocking comment. He disposed of Smoochy's original cookies in the trash can. He later held up the magic cookie bag after stuffing his costume into the duffle bag.

memories.memory_text: I found my old costume in a box labeled 'Rainbow Randolph crap'—I held it close, smelled it, and heard the kids laughing again… I knew I had to come back.
grounded_facts: Randolph discovered a box marked with
Rainbow Randolph crap
in the corner of the prop room. He pulled it out
opened it

and found his old costume inside. He removed the costume and clutched it lovingly. He smelled the fabric. He experienced an internal echo of his theme song and children laughing. He whispered to himself about returning
first softly
then with rising anger.

memories.memory_text: They grabbed me, those three brutes in uniform, and just started dragging me down the stairs—kicking, punching, throwing me around like I was nothing. I remember the cold metal of the door hitting my back right before they hurled me out.
grounded_facts: Three Kidnet security guards physically dragged Randolph down the stairwell
The guards beat Randolph severely during the expulsion
Randolph was thrown through an exit door at the end of the stairwell

memories.scene_title: 25、INT. FRANK STOKES' OFFICE - TIGHT ON NEW YORK POST FRONT
memories.memory_text: I remember the headline—'Rainbow Randolph Interrupts Smoochy Taping'—and those words burned into my eyes, reducing me to a joke, a punchline about penises while that rhino sits there, patted like a pet.
grounded_facts: The New York Post headline reads: 'Rainbow Randolph Interrupts Smoochy Taping.'
The subhead states: 'Runs Onstage Shouting Penis Related Obscenities.'
Burke and Sheldon are present, with Burke reading the paper and reacting with disgust.

memories.memory_text: I kicked the door open and tackled Angelo—he was supposed to be loyal, a Krinkle Kid like me, not some rhino groupie.
grounded_facts: Randolph kicked open the door and tackled Angelo. Randolph accused Angelo of being a traitor for working with Smoochy. Randolph demanded Angelo declare himself a Krinkle Kid. Randolph invoked religious oaths for Angelo to confess loyalty. Randolph believed Angelo had betrayed their shared past.

memories.memory_text: After he punched me and put a gun to my face, I started crying—I just wanted to know if I could stay, I had nowhere else.
grounded_facts: Angelo punched Randolph in the mouth and overpowered him. Angelo pointed a revolver at Randolph's face. Randolph began crying uncontrollably. Randolph asked to stay in Angelo's apartment. Randolph expressed having no other place to go. Randolph displayed visible emotional breakdown after physical defeat.

memories.memory_text: I showed Angelo the bump on my eye and pulled back my lip to show him the gap where my tooth used to be—told him network security did that when I went back to the studio. I spat the bay leaf on the floor and drank straight from the gin bottle, telling him what they sowed, they’d reap—I meant every word.
grounded_facts: Randolph points to a bump near his eye and attributes it to being kicked out of the corporate penthouse. He yanks his lip back to reveal a missing tooth
blaming network security for the injury. He spits a bay leaf on the floor while eating soup and drinks from a gin bottle. He says
What dey sow, dey gonna reap,
in a mocking dialect before passing out. His tone is vengeful and bitter as he lists his losses and injuries.

memories.memory_text: I asked Sheldon if he took glee in stealing Rainbow Randolph's time slot and shoveling dirt on his corpse, pushing him to admit he hated the man.
grounded_facts: Randolph, in disguise, refers to Rainbow Randolph in the third person as a scoundrel.
He asks Sheldon if he takes pleasure in Rainbow Randolph's downfall, using the metaphor of shoveling dirt on his corpse.
Randolph presses Sheldon to admit he hates Rainbow Randolph.

memories.memory_text: I snapped at Sheldon, yelling that Rainbow Randolph was a fucking asshole and demanding he admit he hated him—only to be met with silence and discomfort when he changed the subject to the radio.
grounded_facts: Randolph becomes frustrated and eventually snaps, shouting that Rainbow Randolph is a fucking asshole.
He demands that Sheldon admit he hates Rainbow Randolph.
Sheldon responds with discomfort and deflects by asking to listen to the radio.

memories.memory_text: I walked into Nora's place, threw off my jacket, and told her the R man was back—ready to whistle happy tunes for the little chillens, starting Monday. I flopped down on her couch like I owned the place, demanding a Scotch like nothing had changed.
grounded_facts: Randolph enters Nora's apartment uninvited and removes his jacket.
He declares his return from sabbatical and demands his old job back.
He references calling wardrobe and props, and says he'll start Monday.
He sits on the couch and asks for a Scotch.

memories.memory_text: She got in my face, accusing me of setting up Sheldon, backing me against the wall—her eyes locked on mine like a cop. I didn't answer, just threw back that she was always a bitch with accusations, and then—bam—the next thing I felt was her fist cracking my jaw.
grounded_facts: Nora confronts Randolph about being at the rally and setting up Sheldon.
She backs him against the wall and demands an answer.
Randolph responds with defiance and insults her as a 'bitch'.
Nora punches him in the jaw.

memories.memory_text: I stood there with my lawyer, bloodied but still proud, and told those reporters to their faces — being hated in this country is a badge of honor, not a shame.
grounded_facts: Randolph emerged from the police station bruised and with his lawyer
Reporters asked him about being voted the most hated man in America
Randolph responded defiantly, calling the public 'Neanderthals' and wearing the hatred as a badge of honor

memories.memory_text: An egg hit me right in the face — cold, slimy — and I couldn't hold it back, I just shouted it was unfair, that it was mean, I was on the verge of tears.
grounded_facts: Someone in the crowd threw an egg that struck Randolph in the face
Randolph reacted emotionally, saying 'That was unfair!' and 'That was mean and uncalled for!'
He was on the verge of tears, showing visible emotional distress

memories.memory_text: I was just sitting there, cheese curls in my lap, and then I saw him—Smoochy—on the screen, grinning like some horned saint while everyone cheered. The pressure in my head built until I couldn't think, just feel the rage burning behind my eyes.
grounded_facts: Randolph was sitting in front of the TV wearing a ratty bathrobe with a bag of cheese curls on his lap.
He was watching a broadcast showing Smoochy's triumphant return with public adoration and media praise.
He mumbled to himself about pressure building, ears ringing, eyes burning, and contempt overflowing before attacking the TV.

memories.memory_text: After I kicked the TV and swung the lamp, Angelo came running, took it from me, and then pointed at the door—told me to get out. I barely got out 'Where am I supposed to go?' before he said he didn't care.
grounded_facts: Randolph kicked the TV and used a lamp to beat it before Angelo intervened.
Angelo grabbed the lamp and told Randolph to leave the apartment.
Randolph asked 'Where am I supposed to go?' in a scared tone as Angelo expelled him.

memories.memory_text: I saw that guy with his stupid rhino picture and pamphlets, and when he asked me to donate, something snapped—I jumped over the table and grabbed him, screaming that I'm the one who needs saving, that I'm broke and homeless and a peanut shell in the shape of a man.
grounded_facts: Randolph sees a man with a table displaying rhino imagery and donation materials.
The man asks Randolph to help save the rhino.
Randolph reacts by attacking the man, shouting about being broke, losing his lawyer, being kicked out, and feeling like a 'peanut shell in the shape of a man'.
Randolph explicitly shouts 'I'm the one who needs to be saved!'

memories.memory_text: After screaming at that guy, I grabbed the metal donation can off his table and ran, yelling 'Save the Rainbow!' over and over, my slippers slapping the pavement as I fled.
grounded_facts: Randolph grabs the collection can from the activist's table.
He flees the scene immediately after the attack.
He chants 'Save the Rainbow!' while running away.
He is wearing ratty bedroom slippers during the incident.

memories.scene_title: 74、EXT. TIMES SQUARE - OUTSIDE KIDNET BUILDING - TIGHT
memories.memory_text: I stood on that table in Times Square, raised the gasoline can high, and shouted to the crowd about the injustice Smoochy brought on me—then I poured it all over myself, the cold shock of it soaking through my clothes, clinging to my skin.
grounded_facts: Randolph stands on a small card table outside the Kidnet Building and addresses a growing crowd using a megaphone. He accuses Smoochy the Rhino of slandering and defaming him. He picks up a can of gasoline
declares that only he can take his life
and drenches himself with the liquid. The act is performed in front of an audience
and he does so dramatically
pausing before dousing himself.

memories.scene_title: 74、EXT. TIMES SQUARE - OUTSIDE KIDNET BUILDING - TIGHT
memories.memory_text: After I soaked myself, I held up the pack of matches for everyone to see—my hand steady despite the fear, a final warning meant for Smoochy’s eyes alone, a fire waiting to happen if he didn’t show.
grounded_facts: Randolph holds up a pack of matches after dousing himself with gasoline. He states the act is intended for Smoochy to witness and remember. The crowd begins applauding
misinterpreting the gesture as performance. He tells the crowd not to talk him out of it
indicating intent to follow through.

memories.memory_text: I finally got the match lit, held it up like a torch for the world to see — and then this little girl just walks up and blows it out, smiling like it was a birthday candle.
grounded_facts: Randolph successfully lights a match after multiple attempts
a little girl approaches him during the moment of ignition
she blows out the match while smiling
the crowd falls silent during the interaction

memories.memory_text: I was still standing there, the match gone, the girl vanished — and then the limo roared past me, the crowd screaming for Smoochy like I wasn't even there. I dropped to my knees and screamed into the sky, but no one looked back.
grounded_facts: Sheldon's limo passes Randolph without stopping
the crowd abandons Randolph to rush toward the limo
Sheldon exits the vehicle to cheers
Randolph drops to his knees and screams in anguish
the camera rises to show him isolated amid the skyscrapers

memories.memory_text: I was begging Angelo on the pay phone in Times Square, telling him I didn't do it, that I was soaked in gasoline and people thought I was crazy, but he still wouldn't let me come back—he said I had to go underground.
grounded_facts: Randolph is on a pay phone in Times Square at night.
He is speaking to Angelo, who is on the other end of the line.
He denies involvement in the boxer's murder.
He references being drenched in gasoline and acting peculiar.
Angelo says he believes Randolph but refuses to let him return, warning that he'll be found and must go underground.

memories.scene_title: 89、INT. LIBERTY BISCUIT AND FLOUR FACTORY - CONTINUOUS
memories.memory_text: I told Angelo it was time to heal, to accept that Smoochy had won and that I’d move forward with faith—justice would prevail, I said, even though I’m wanted for a murder I didn’t commit.
grounded_facts: Randolph expresses a desire to heal and accept Smoochy's victory. He mentions having faith that justice will prevail despite being falsely accused. He speaks about his past as an altar boy and belief in angels. He rests his head on Angelo's shoulder and receives comfort. Angelo praises him for making a positive emotional step. Randolph asks for lunch and manages a small smile when given chicken and stars soup.

memories.scene_title: 89、INT. LIBERTY BISCUIT AND FLOUR FACTORY - CONTINUOUS
memories.memory_text: I saw them in the newspaper—Sheldon and Nora, arms around each other at Spinner’s wake—and something inside me snapped. I screamed so loud the birds flew from the rafters.
grounded_facts: Randolph sees a photo in the New York Post of Sheldon and Nora with their arms around each other at Spinner's wake. His eyes widen and he begins to hyperventilate upon seeing the image. He lets out a blood-curdling scream that startles Angelo and causes birds to scatter. The scream echoes through the factory
marking a complete emotional collapse. The moment directly follows his earlier resolve to move forward.

memories.memory_text: I was lounging on Sheldon's sofa, fresh out of the shower in his robe, eating grapes and flipping through National Geographic when the phone rang—I picked it up singing, not a care in the world.
grounded_facts: Randolph was wearing Sheldon's robe and had a pink towel on his head.
He was eating grapes and reading National Geographic.
He answered the phone in a sing-songy, playful tone.

memories.memory_text: When Angelo said Buggy Double D was working for Burke Bennett, something snapped—I felt it in my gut. I tore the towel off my head and knew I had to get to the Garden, cops or not. I have to warn the rhino.
grounded_facts: Randolph became visibly worried after hearing about Buggy Double D and Burke Bennett.
He expressed concern for Sheldon's safety.
He declared he didn't care about the police and insisted on warning Smoochy.
He physically removed the towel from his head in a sudden, dramatic motion.

memories.memory_text: I was carrying Smoochy’s body over my shoulder and his head in a bag when she introduced herself—then I saw her card said 'Nora Bishop. V.P. of Development. Kidnet.' I remember thinking, Good gravy. You work for Kidnet? before shaking her hand too hard, too fast.
grounded_facts: Sheldon was in street clothes, carrying Smoochy's costume on a hanger and the head in a shopping bag.
Nora Bishop introduced herself and handed Sheldon her business card.
Sheldon reacted verbally with 'Good gravy. You work for Kidnet?' upon reading her title and company.
Sheldon grabbed and shook Nora's hand immediately after reading the card.

memories.memory_text: I tried to tell Nora how much I’ve contributed—the two thousand songs, the messages behind them—hoping she’d see me as more than just a costume, but she didn’t even pretend to care.
grounded_facts: Sheldon mentions creating two thousand original Smoochy songs covering topics like vegetables and plasma donation.
He explicitly states his desire for the show to have substance and sees himself as a valuable resource.
Nora responds by indicating her complete lack of interest.

memories.memory_text: When she stopped and looked at me, I thought maybe she’d finally listen—instead, she said I was just a delivery, like groceries, and that my only job was to smile and nod.
grounded_facts: Nora stops walking and looks Sheldon in the eye for the first time in the scene.
She tells Sheldon he is only on TV because Rainbow Randolph is disgraced.
She compares delivering him to delivering a bag of groceries and says he should just smile and nod.

memories.memory_text: I held that crumpled show rundown and asked Nora why they cut the 'Please and Thank You Song'—how could they remove the one part that actually taught the kids something?
grounded_facts: Sheldon is in the Smoochy costume (head off) and holds a show rundown.
He directly questions Nora about the removal of the 'Please and Thank You Song'.
He argues that the song provides a moral lesson and calls it the 'moral anchor'.

memories.memory_text: I turned back and told her, 'When I go out there I see kids. You see wallets with pigtails'—the words just came out, but I knew they cut deeper than any contract.
grounded_facts: After being dismissed, Sheldon stops, turns back, and delivers the line about seeing kids versus wallets with pigtails.
The line is spoken directly to Nora as a moral rebuke.
The moment has a noticeable impact—Nora stares, clearly affected.

memories.scene_title: 26、INT. SPINNER DUNN'S RESTAURANT - CLOSE ON CHAMPAGNE
memories.memory_text: I couldn't believe it—creative control, part ownership, a dressing room with a toilet. I kept asking myself if I was dreaming, it felt too good to be real.
grounded_facts: Sheldon expresses shock and disbelief at his promotion. He lists specific benefits: creative control
part ownership
merchandising discretion
and a private toilet. He questions whether the moment is real
indicating emotional overwhelm. The champagne toast with Burke marks the celebratory context. Sheldon verbally reacts with childlike wonder and gratitude.

memories.scene_title: 26、INT. SPINNER DUNN'S RESTAURANT - CLOSE ON CHAMPAGNE
memories.memory_text: He handed me a gun in a paper bag, and when I pulled it out, I flinched—I just dropped it. I told him I'd never touched a gun, that I don't believe in them.
grounded_facts: Burke gives Sheldon a paper bag as a gift. Sheldon reaches into the bag and pulls out a revolver. He immediately recoils and drops the gun back into the bag. He states his personal history and moral stance against guns. His physical reaction indicates visceral discomfort.

memories.memory_text: I saw my face on the cover of Variety, smiling back at me under the headline that said I was starting today as executive producer — then I paid for the magazine, looked up at the Kidnet building, and walked inside, breathing deep like I was stepping into something real.
grounded_facts: Sheldon's picture appears on the front page of Variety with the headline 'MOPES TO EXECUTIVE PRODUCE SMOOCHY SHOW' and subhead 'TENURE TO BEGIN TODAY'.
Sheldon buys the magazine from a newsstand in front of the Kidnet Building.
Sheldon looks up at the Kidnet building, smiles, takes a deep breath, and enters.

memories.memory_text: I remember the heavy thud of the wad of cash hitting my desk, how Ben just dropped it there like it was nothing—like my integrity was for sale. I picked it up and threw it back at him, told him I’d make an exception to take the trash out early.
grounded_facts: Ben Franks placed a large wad of money on Sheldon's desk during their meeting.
The money landed with a noticeable thud.
Sheldon expressed discomfort and questioned if it was a bribe.
Sheldon picked up the money and tossed it back to Ben.
Sheldon referred to Ben as trash and escorted him out of the office.

memories.memory_text: I tore the contract in half right there at the table—felt the paper split in my hands—and told Burke that Smoochy doesn't sell out, not like those guys who paint driveways black and call it paving.
grounded_facts: Sheldon tears the contract in half during a conversation with Burke at Spinner Dunn's.
Sheldon states that ice shows represent mindless spectacles that prioritize profit over integrity.
Sheldon references his childhood vow not to make a living through deception, symbolized by the image of painting driveways instead of properly paving them.

memories.memory_text: I told Spinner his performance was great, but I had to mention his elbow—he nearly knocked out Pinky’s tooth.
grounded_facts: Sheldon praised Spinner's performance.
Sheldon gave feedback about Spinner's elbow.

memories.memory_text: When Nora called Spinner a mental patient, I snapped—I had to defend him. He’s not broken, he’s just gentle, with a sweet innocent brain like a five-year-old.
grounded_facts: Nora made a dismissive comment about casting mental patients.
Sheldon corrected her, emphasizing Spinner's identity and dignity.
Sheldon used the phrase 'sweet innocent brain of a five-year-old.'

memories.memory_text: I was just trying to hail a cab after leaving the Kidnet building when a man in a suit smiled and told me to get in the limo—he seemed friendly at first, but then he showed me a gun and called me 'rhino' like it was some kind of joke.
grounded_facts: Sheldon was exiting the Kidnet building and heading to the curb to get a cab.
Merv Green approached him and opened the door of a limo.
When Sheldon declined, Merv revealed a revolver tucked in his waistband.
Merv said 'Humor me, rhino' as a coercive statement.

memories.memory_text: Merv Green sat across from me in the limo, smiling like this was some kind of business meeting, and told me Smoochy was starting tomorrow — benefits, banquets, all of it — like I had any choice in the matter.
grounded_facts: Merv Green introduced himself as representing the Parade of Hope Foundation.
Merv stated that Smoochy would begin performing for Parade of Hope starting the next day, listing benefits, banquets, and fund-raisers.
The statement was delivered after Sheldon refused involvement and asked to be let out.

memories.memory_text: I remember the door suddenly popping open, and then being shoved out into the street — just tossed like garbage — while the limo idled at the curb, Merv’s voice still hanging in the air about ice skates.
grounded_facts: After Sheldon refused to participate, Merv told the driver to pull over.
The limo door opened unexpectedly, and Sheldon was physically ejected by the thug (Henry) or Merv’s order.
The ejection occurred immediately after Merv’s comment about shopping for skates.

memories.memory_text: I remember doing the Klunky-Wunky dance with Nora, laughing at how ridiculous it was, and then suddenly kissing her—really kissing her—after years of feeling like no one truly got why Smoochy mattered.
grounded_facts: Sheldon mentions Rickets the Hippo and connects it to Smoochy. Nora shares that Rickets inspired her too. They both perform the Klunky-Wunky dance together. Sheldon initiates a kiss
then apologizes
but Nora pulls him into a passionate kiss. Nora questions his authenticity during the kiss
and he affirms he’s genuine.

memories.memory_text: Right after Nora left, the phone rang—some call I had to take, I guess, though I just wanted to stay in that moment, still tasting orange juice and wondering if any of it really happened.
grounded_facts: The phone rings immediately after Nora says 'Good night' and exits. Sheldon sighs
closes the door
and moves toward the bar. He pours himself orange juice before answering the phone. The call interrupts the emotional aftermath of the kiss. He does not know who is calling
but answers anyway.

memories.memory_text: I stepped up to the mic, heart pounding, staring at that massive swastika behind me while hundreds of Nazis chanted my name—'Heil Smoochy!'—and I could barely breathe. All I managed to say was, 'It's my sincere hope that I'm dreaming right now... what's the deal with the swastikas?'
grounded_facts: Sheldon hears the crowd chanting 'Heil Smoochy' in a warehouse filled with Nazis.
A large swastika banner unfurls behind him.
He approaches the microphone, clears his throat, and speaks the line about hoping he's dreaming and questioning the swastikas.

memories.memory_text: One second I was standing there, frozen, trying to make sense of the nightmare, and then suddenly cops stormed in, yelling, and chaos exploded all around me. I didn't move—I just stayed in the middle, completely unsure what to do as Nazis scattered like roaches.
grounded_facts: Police burst into the warehouse and declare the assembly unlawful.
The lead cop yells at the Nazis, calling them 'kraut sons-a-bitches'.
Pandemonium breaks out as people run, but Sheldon remains in place, disoriented.

memories.memory_text: I was in handcuffs, flashing lights all around, and reporters shouting—asking if I was part of the Aryan Brotherhood, if I admired Hitler. I kept saying it was a mix-up, that I didn’t hate anyone, but they twisted my words.
grounded_facts: Sheldon is in handcuffs and being led from the warehouse. Reporters are shouting questions about the Aryan Brotherhood and Adolf Hitler. Flashbulbs are going off. Sheldon protests
saying he's innocent and doesn't hate anyone. A reporter misrepresents his statement by asking if he doesn't hate Nazis.

memories.memory_text: I was cut off mid-sentence, trying to explain, and then suddenly shoved into the back of a squad car—the door slammed, the sirens started, and we sped off before I could say anything more.
grounded_facts: Sheldon is interrupted while speaking ("Wait! I didn't say that! I --"). He is physically shoved into the back seat of a squad car. The car drives away with sirens blaring. The moment marks an abrupt end to his attempt to respond.

memories.memory_text: I remember sitting in that precinct chair, hands pressed into cold ink, staring ahead as the camera flashed—then suddenly I was back in the studio, Smoochy spinning with a child in his arms, laughing, the jungle set bright and alive… and then it was gone.
grounded_facts: Sheldon was fingerprinted and photographed at the police precinct.
A flashback shows Smoochy dancing in the studio with a child.
The scene transitions between police processing and fragmented memories.

memories.memory_text: There was no sound, no heat—just the costume hanging in black space, flames curling around the fur, the colors melting into ash. I couldn’t move. I just watched it burn, like it was me inside that suit.
grounded_facts: The Smoochy costume is shown burning in a black limbo space.
The burning occurs in a surreal, isolated visual plane.
The destruction of the costume is presented as symbolic and final.

memories.memory_text: I stood there in my knit cap and sunglasses, heart pounding, begging Nora to believe me when I said I didn’t know it was a Nazi rally—she just kept walking, like my words didn’t even matter.
grounded_facts: Sheldon approached Nora while disguised with a knit cap and sunglasses.
He insisted he had no idea the event was a Nazi rally.
Nora dismissed his explanation, referencing the swastika in the photo.

memories.memory_text: When I reminded her about the balcony and the Klunky-Wunky dance, hoping it meant something, she said she was drunk and not to read into it—I felt like the ground vanished beneath me.
grounded_facts: Sheldon referenced shared intimate moments (balcony, Klunky-Wunky dance) to appeal to Nora.
Nora responded that she was drunk and those moments were meaningless.
Sheldon appeared visibly crushed after her response.

memories.scene_title: 57、EXT. SIDE STREET (CONEY ISLAND) - FEW MINUTES LATER
memories.memory_text: I stood there staring at the boarded-up clinic, the place where I used to perform for those trying to get clean, and when the old man said, 'Hell of a world,' it echoed something I already felt—like everything I believed in had just been sealed behind plywood and forgotten.
grounded_facts: Sheldon is standing in front of a closed, boarded-up methadone clinic.
An old vagrant approaches and informs him the clinic has been shut down by the city.
The vagrant expresses sorrow and frustration about societal priorities, then says 'Hell of a world.'
Sheldon repeats the phrase 'Hell of a world' to himself after the vagrant leaves.

memories.scene_title: 63、EXT. TIMES SQUARE - SERIES OF DISSOLVES - BEFORE DAWN
memories.memory_text: I stood there in the Smoochy suit, watching the headlines spill onto the curb—my name in print, people shouting 'We're sorry, Smoochy!'—and for the first time, I wasn't the villain.
grounded_facts: Headlines in the morning editions reveal that Smiley orchestrated a Nazi plot and set up Smoochy.
Public opinion shifts dramatically, with a poll naming Rainbow Randolph the most hated man in America.
There is a public outcry expressing regret toward Smoochy, symbolized by the phrase 'We're sorry Smoochy!'
The scene takes place in Times Square before dawn, with newspaper bundles being thrown onto the curb.

memories.memory_text: I remember standing backstage, the makeup woman touching up my forehead, and Nora beside me—her voice soft with regret as she said she was sorry she hadn’t believed me. I told her this town’s like a house of mirrors; it’s hard to know what you’re seeing.
grounded_facts: Sheldon is backstage at Studio C, about to go on stage.
A makeup woman is dabbing his forehead.
Nora is standing next to him.
Nora apologizes for not believing him and being swept up in anti-Smoochy sentiment.
Sheldon responds by comparing the town to a house of mirrors.
He takes deep breaths before being called on stage by a stagehand.

memories.memory_text: I stood in the black limbo light, holding the microphone, telling the kids the jungle isn't what it seems—how fairness doesn't always win, how sweet things can have poison underneath. Their gasps were sharp, like they’d never heard truth like that before.
grounded_facts: Sheldon, as Smoochy, delivers a serious message about injustice and deception in the jungle.
He speaks from a darkened stage (black limbo) after a dramatic entrance.
He references lollipop trees with roots in venom and warns children to be vigilant.
The children react with gasps and then howl along when prompted.

memories.memory_text: After the weight of it all, I just asked them, 'Who wants to do the Hokey-Pokey?'—and the moment the music started, I was spinning and laughing, pulling kids onto the set, dancing like nothing else mattered. I saw Nora clapping, and for a second, everything felt light again.
grounded_facts: Sheldon, as Smoochy, transitions from his serious speech to initiating the Hokey-Pokey.
The Smoochyland Band plays the Hokey-Pokey song.
Children rush from the bleachers to dance with him on the jungle set.
Nora is seen clapping offstage, and Sheldon shares a glance with her.

memories.memory_text: I stood up at Spinner Dunn's and declared I'd do the ice show my way—no sponsors, no vendors, no one profiting off these kids—and that every dollar would go to build a methadone palace in Coney Island, a place where people could withdraw in dignity.
grounded_facts: Sheldon announces his decision to perform in the ice show.
He states that no sponsors, vendors, or 'dirty hands' will be involved.
He dedicates all profits to building a state-of-the-art methadone clinic in Coney Island.
He describes the clinic as a 'methadone palace' where people can withdraw in luxury.
He asserts personal agency, saying he's no longer letting the business use him.

memories.memory_text: I remember sitting in the limo, the paper in my hands showing the headline about the ice show at the Garden, all proceeds going to the Brooklyn Clinic, and telling Lester that power shouldn't be squandered—that it should be used to build something real.
grounded_facts: Sheldon is in the back of a limo reading a newspaper.
The newspaper headline announces an ice show at Madison Square Garden with proceeds benefiting the Brooklyn Clinic.
Sheldon speaks to the driver, Lester, saying 'Use your power. Don't squander it -- build with it.'

memories.memory_text: I introduced Spinner as Moochy the Rhino on live TV, and right after, he leaned in and whispered that the costume was making his nuts itch—I felt my stomach drop, horrified, but had to keep dancing through it.
grounded_facts: Sheldon, as Smoochy, publicly introduced Spinner Dunn as the new character Moochy the Rhino during a live taping.
Spinner whispered to Sheldon (in character) that the costume was making his 'nuts itch'.
Sheldon reacted with visible horror, but the band continued playing and the performance carried on with dancing.

memories.scene_title: 79、INT. SHELDON'S DRESSING ROOM - AFTER THE SHOW
memories.memory_text: I was exhausted after the taping, still in the Smoochy suit, when Nora came in and started asking about us—about the distance. I didn’t know what to say, I just felt this pressure building, and then I grabbed her and kissed her, like that was the only way to make it real.
grounded_facts: Sheldon was in his dressing room after a difficult taping, still wearing the Smoochy costume.
Nora entered and expressed concern about emotional disconnection in their relationship.
Sheldon felt uncomfortable and struggled to respond verbally.
He suddenly kissed Nora passionately, initiating physical intimacy instead of continuing the conversation.

memories.memory_text: I stood there looking at Spinner in the casket, his hand still wrapped around that cowbell, and I couldn’t hold it back—I told him I was sorry, over and over, like he could hear me.
grounded_facts: Sheldon stands at Spinner's open casket and apologizes to him.
Spinner is depicted as sleeping forever, clutching a cowbell.
Sheldon expresses guilt, stating it was his fault and that Spinner would still be alive if not for him.

memories.memory_text: Nora started singing that old Rickets the Hippo song, 'The Last Nappy Time,' so quiet, and I joined in—our voices together, just barely above a whisper, like a secret between us.
grounded_facts: Nora sings 'The Last Nappy Time' quietly after the wake conversation.
Sheldon joins Nora in singing the song.
They embrace after singing, while bagpipe music swells in the background.
